title: Most people get these 3 AI terms wrong
summary: Jeff Su clarifies three commonly misunderstood AI terms. Hallucination shares mechanisms with creativity and differs by task needs. Context window works as a fixed-size buffer that drops old information when full. Tokens are AI processing chunks whose usage drives plan limitations.
writeup: A short explainer defining three foundational AI terms for everyday users: hallucination, context window, and tokens. The central reframe is that hallucination is not a defect in isolation but the mechanical flip side of creativity, making the real question one of task fit rather than model failure. The other two definitions give practical diagnostic heuristics: start a new chat when answers degrade, and prompt efficiently to extend free-tier usage.

key_points: Hallucination is the flip side of creativity: disabling web search in ChatGPT or Claude trades accuracy for more creativity, and enabling it trades creativity for more accuracy, so neither mode is universally better.
The context window behaves like a fixed-size whiteboard: once full, the AI cannot add new information without discarding earlier content, which explains degrading answers over a long chat.
AI breaks input into chunks called tokens, and every token costs usage quota, so tighter prompting lets users do more before hitting free-plan limits.
